coredata.dc:title: Improving photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanorods: A comparison between thermal decomposition of zinc acetate under vacuum and in ambient air
coredata.dc:creator: Rahimi, Kourosh
Yazdani, Ahmad
coredata.dc:description: Zinc oxide (ZnO) nanorods were synthesized by thermal decomposition of zinc acetate dihydrate, once under vacuum and once in ambient air. The structures were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and X-ray diffractometry (XRD), showing that thermal decomposition in ambient air, as compared to thermal decomposition under vacuum, increases the crystallinity of the obtained ZnO nanorods and yields non-aggregated nanorods. Optical bandgaps of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air were estimated as 3.3 and 3.1 eV, respectively. The sample synthesized in ambient air had also a higher absorption below 450 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectrometry indicates that thermal decomposition in ambient air slightly redshifts the band-to-band peak of ZnO nanorods and also decreases the PL intensity; however, the ratio of the intensities of the band-to-band peak to the green peak increases from 1.33 to 3.05, indicating the higher crystallinity and the fewer defects of the sample synthesized in ambient air. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area measurements indicated that the sample synthesized in ambient air has a more surface area, about 1.27 times than the sample synthesized under vacuum. Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue was analyzed as a measure of the photocatalytic activity of samples, indicating that the sample synthesized in ambient air has a 2.77 times higher photocatalytic activity than the sample synthesized under vacuum, with a more stable photocatalytic activity. Finally, the mechanism behind the enhanced photocatalytic activity was discussed.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc oxide
Nanorods
Photocatalyst
Crystallinity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank research council of Tarbiat Modares University for providing financial support. The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FESEM images of ZnO NRs synthesized (a, b) under vacuum and (c, d) in ambient air.
XRD patterns of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air.

UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra and absorption spectra (Kubelka-Munk function) of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air. The corresponding direct-bandgap Tauc plots, obtained from the Kubelka-Munk function, are also shown in the inset.
PL spectra of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air, with the excitation wavelength of 325 nm.
Nitrogen adsorption–desorption BET isotherms of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air.
Time-varied absorption spectra of MB solutions being photocatalytically degraded by ZnO nanorods synthesized (a) under vacuum and (b) in ambient air for different times.

(Left) Time-varied photocatalytic decolorization of methylene blue by photolysis, commercial ZnO powder, ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum, and ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air. (Right) Plots of Ln(C0/Ct) versus time for the corresponding data in the time-varied degradation data.
Recyclability of photocatalytic activities of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air for six successive cycles.

head.ce:title: Improving photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanorods: A comparison between thermal decomposition of zinc acetate under vacuum and in ambient air

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Correspondence to: Condensed Matter Group, Department of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Jalal-Ale-Ahmad Avenue, Tehran, Iran.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Condensed Matter Group, Department of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Zinc oxide (ZnO) nanorods were synthesized by thermal decomposition of zinc acetate dihydrate, once under vacuum and once in ambient air. The structures were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) and X-ray diffractometry (XRD), showing that thermal decomposition in ambient air, as compared to thermal decomposition under vacuum, increases the crystallinity of the obtained ZnO nanorods and yields non-aggregated nanorods. Optical bandgaps of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air were estimated as 3.3 and 3.1 eV, respectively. The sample synthesized in ambient air had also a higher absorption below 450 nm. Photoluminescence (PL) spectrometry indicates that thermal decomposition in ambient air slightly redshifts the band-to-band peak of ZnO nanorods and also decreases the PL intensity; however, the ratio of the intensities of the band-to-band peak to the green peak increases from 1.33 to 3.05, indicating the higher crystallinity and the fewer defects of the sample synthesized in ambient air. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area measurements indicated that the sample synthesized in ambient air has a more surface area, about 1.27 times than the sample synthesized under vacuum. Photocatalytic degradation of methylene blue was analyzed as a measure of the photocatalytic activity of samples, indicating that the sample synthesized in ambient air has a 2.77 times higher photocatalytic activity than the sample synthesized under vacuum, with a more stable photocatalytic activity. Finally, the mechanism behind the enhanced photocatalytic activity was discussed.

ce:section.ce:para: A major contaminant that is largely found in industrial wastewater, like in textiles and cosmetics, is dyes, such as methylene blue. The dyes are detrimental to environment and people. A most-promising method to decrease the traces of the dyes is photocatalysis. Photocatalysts are also used for degrading living organisms such as bacteria and minuscule animals [1]. Photocatalysis is the process of catalyzing a reaction under irradiation, and it is mainly utilized for hydrogen production through water splitting and degradation of harmful organic materials [2–9]. Metal oxide semiconductors, such as ZnO and TiO2, can generate electron-hole pairs under irradiation, and the pairs are required for redox reactions that play an important role in photocatalysis processes. However, metal oxide semiconductors suffer from two main disadvantages in the field of photocatalysis: (1) they have a large bandgap (mainly in the UV region), so that only a limited region of sunlight can be utilized, and (2) they have a high rate of electron-hole pair recombination, so that the generated electrons and holes remain active only for a limited time. Zinc oxide is a naturally n-type semiconductor with a direct, wide bandgap of ~ 3.37 eV and a high excitonic binding energy of ~ 60 meV [10–12], and it can stably exist in various forms. ZnO nanostructures have been widely investigated as a photocatalyst [12,13]. There are a variety of techniques to synthesize ZnO nanostructures. Thermal decomposition of zinc acetate [14] is a one-pot, facile, straightforward, inexpensive, and large-scalable method to synthesize ZnO nanorods.

Thermal decomposition of zinc acetate was first developed by Lin et al. [14–16] to grow vertically aligned ZnO nanorods on indium tin oxide coated substrates and also substrate-free ZnO nanorods for field emission and UV sensing applications. Later, the ZnO nanorods were synthesized at various temperatures and for various times for photocatalyst applications [17–21]. Zhang et al. [19] studied the effect of aspect ratio on the photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanorods synthesized by mechanical-assisted thermal decomposition of zinc acetate, concluding that the higher aspect ratio the higher photocatalytic activity. Punithavathy et al. [18] studied the photodegradation of methyl violet dye using ZnO nanorods synthesized by thermal decomposition of zinc acetate for different durations (3–12 h). R. Saravanan et al. [20] investigated the photocatalytic activity of ZnO synthesized by thermal decomposition of zinc acetate at various temperatures (350, 650, and 950 °C) for degradation of methyl orange and methylene blue dyes, concluding that the sample synthesized above 350 °C has a larger crystallite size and a weaker photocatalytic activity, and they also showed that the ZnO nanorods have a higher photocatalytic activity on methylene blue than on methyl orange. Tian et al. [21] investigated the photodegradation of dyes (methyl orange, rhodamine B, and methylene blue) by ZnO synthesized by thermal decomposition of zinc acetate at different temperatures (400, 600, 800, and 900 °C), concluding that the sample synthesized at 600 °C has the highest photocatalytic activity. Richard et al. [17] studied the effect of morphology on photocatalytic degradation of methyl violet dye using ZnO synthesized by thermal decomposition of zinc acetate at different annealing temperatures (250–400 °C).

Here, ZnO nanorods were prepared simply by thermal decomposition of zinc acetate dihydrate. However, to the best of our knowledge, it is for the first time that thermal decomposition of zinc acetate dihydrate under vacuum and in ambient air is investigated for comparing the photocatalytic activities of the prepared ZnO nanorods. Photocatalytic decolorization of methylene blue, as a model dye, was tested to analyze the photocatalytic activities of the structures. Finally, the mechanism behind the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the ZnO nanorods prepared in ambient air was discussed.

Zinc oxide nanorods were simply prepared via thermal decomposition of zinc acetate dihydrate. In a typical experiment, 0.5 g zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(O2CCH5)2(H2O)2) (Merck, 99.5%) was placed in a crucible and it was heated to 300 °C in a furnace and maintained at the temperature for 3 h, once under vacuum and once in ambient air. Finally, the obtained samples were washed three times with ethanol and deionized water.

Photocatalytic decolorization of methylene blue (MB) solution by the prepared samples was monitored to study the photocatalytic activities of the samples. A 50 ml aqueous solution containing 2.5 × 10−5 M methylene blue was prepared. Next, 20 mg of the prepared powder was added, and the obtained mixture was maintained in the dark for 1 h to achieve an equilibrium between adsorption and desorption processes. A 250 W metal-halide light bulb (Philips) was used to irradiate the mixtures, and it was adjusted above the beaker to obtain the irradiance of ~ 1000 W/m2 to simulate the solar power. In order to eliminate thermal effects arising from the surrounding environment and the lamp, a water circulator was used to maintain the mixtures at the room temperature. The absorption of the MB solution at its highest absorption peak (665 nm) was monitored every 30 min, totally for 5 h. All photocatalyst tests were repeated for three times in total, and the reported values are their average.

Crystallographic structures of the samples were studied by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X′Pert MPD, Philips) with a Cu-Kα radiation source (λ = 1.5418 Å). Morphologies of the samples were observed by a field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi S-4160). UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the powders were recorded by a Shimadzu spectrophotometer. Absorption spectra of the photocatalytically treated MB solutions were recorded by a double-beam spectrophotometer (Unico, 4802). Room-temperature photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the samples were recorded by a PL spectroscope (Gilden pλotonics, equipped with a xenon lamp). BET surface areas were measured by a multipoint method using the nitrogen adsorption data obtained by a Micromeritics TriStar instrument.

Fig. 1(a), (b) shows FESEM images of the ZnO nanorod powder synthesized by thermal decomposition of zinc acetate dihydrate under vacuum. The sample consists mainly of nanorods with a mean diameter smaller than 100 nm. However, their lengths are not uniform, and they are distributed over 100 nm to 1 µm. It has been suggested that the release of acetic acid during the thermal decomposition of zinc acetate decreases the local pH of reaction sites and helps in the formation of anisotropic ZnO nanorods [22]. It is seen that some structures could not grow very much, which could be due to the insufficient supply of oxygen, the absence of humidity [23], and also the nature of thermal decomposition technique as a bottom-up method. As it is seen in Fig. 1(b), the nanorods are highly aggregated. FESEM images of the ZnO nanorod powder synthesized in ambient air are shown in Fig. 1(c), (d). It is seen that the nanorods in this sample are well separated. Although all the nanorods in the sample are not of an equal length and diameter, no particle-like structure is seen, indicating their higher crystallinity compared to the nanorods synthesized under vacuum. It is seen that ZnO nanorods are formed as clusters. In Fig. 1(c), two clusters of ZnO nanorods are seen: the left one consists mainly of nanorods of a larger length (~ 2 µm) and diameter (~ 130 nm), and the right one consists mainly of nanorods of a smaller length (~ 700 nm) and diameter (~ 40 nm). The phenomenon could be due to the initial spread of zinc acetate dihydrate powder.

Fig. 2 shows XRD patterns of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air, which are similar to patterns reported in the literature [15]. The diffraction peaks of both samples are located at 2θ = 31.1, 33.8, 35.6, 46.9, 55.9, 62.1, 65.5, 67.2, 68.2, 71.9, 76.3° corresponding to (100), (002), (101), (102), (110), (103), (200), (112), (201), (004), and (202) diffraction planes. All diffraction peaks were matched with the JCPDS 01-079-0208 XRD pattern card, corresponding to the hexagonal wurtzite (space group P63mc) of ZnO crystals. Moreover, there are no other peaks related to secondary phases and impurities. Furthermore, it is obvious that the diffraction peaks of ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air are more intense. The crystallite sizes were obtained using the Scherrer's equation D = 0.9λ/βsin(θ), where D is the average crystallite size, λ is the wavelength of incident X-ray (1.5418 Å), β is the line broadening at full width half maximum (FWHM) of the main diffraction peak (101), and θ is the Bragg angle. By applying the Scherrer's formula, the crystallite sizes of ZnO crystals synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air were calculated as ~ 21 and 24 nm, respectively. The larger crystallite size and the more intense diffraction peaks of the sample synthesized in ambient air as compared to the sample synthesized under vacuum suggest its higher crystallinity, consistent with FESEM images.

UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectra of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air are shown in Fig. 3. The reflectance spectra were converted to absorption spectra using the well-known Kubelka-Munk function
(
R )
=
( 1 − R )
2
2 R
, and they are also shown in Fig. 3. Although the absorption bands of both samples lie in the near UV region, it is seen that the absorption band of ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air redshifts to longer wavelengths as compared to that of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum. Moreover, ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air have a higher absorption in the wavelength range of 250–450 nm. In order to compare the bandgap energies of the obtained structures, the Tauc relation was applied to the absorption spectra obtained using the Kubelka-Munk function, as follows [24]:
(1)
F
( R )
× h ν = A
(
h ν −
E
g
)
n
(
n = ½
for
direct
bandgaps
)
where F(R) is the absorption calculated using the Kubelka-Munk function, ν is the frequency of light, h is the Plank constant, Eg is the bandgap, A is a constant, and n depends on the type of bandgap. Since ZnO is a direct-bandgap semiconductor, if we plot a straight line on the linear part of (F(R)×hν)2 versus hν, the intercept of the line with the hν axis is the bandgap, as it is shown in the inset of Fig. 3. Therefore, the bandgap energies of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air were estimated as 3.3 and 3.1 eV, respectively. The decreased bandgap, as well as the increased absorption of the sample synthesized in ambient air, are appropriate for applications that harvest sunlight.

Fig. 4 shows photoluminescence spectra of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air, both with the equal concentration of 3 mg/ml in deionized water, with the excitation wavelength of 325 nm. Since we have used an equal concentration of both samples, we can compare their intensity. Two peaks can be seen in the PL spectra of both samples. The main peak lies in the near UV region and corresponds to the recombination of generated electrons and holes (excitons), and it is the characteristic band-to-band emission of ZnO. The other peak is in the green region, which is mainly attributed to defects, impurities, and singly-ionized vacancies [25–27], because ZnO is natively an n-type semiconductor due to its oxygen vacancies or zinc interstitials [11]. As it is seen, the position of the band-to-band peak of ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air slightly redshifts as compared to ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum from 378 to 384 nm, which is in agreement with the corresponding absorption spectra and bandgap energies (Fig. 3). The bands related to the green emission lie in a nearly similar range in both spectra. The first point is that the overall photoluminescence of the sample synthesized in ambient air is lower than the sample synthesized under vacuum. The second, and more important, point is that the ratio of intensities of the band-to-band peak to the green peak is 1.33 for the sample synthesized under vacuum and is 3.05 for the sample synthesized in ambient air. The phenomenon could be attributed to the presence of fewer defects in the structure of the sample synthesized in ambient air, which is in agreement with FESEM images and XRD patterns.

The BET surface areas of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air were measured from the N2 adsorption data. Fig. 5 shows the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air. Moreover, the BET specific surface areas [28] of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air were calculated as 15.44 and 19.57 m2/g, respectively, indicating that the ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air have a higher specific surface area than ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum.

In order to compare the photocatalytic activities of the samples, photocatalytic decolorization of methylene blue was analyzed. The details regarding the photocatalyst tests are presented in the experimental section. Fig. 6 shows the time-varies absorption spectra of methylene blue solutions, being photocatalytically degraded by ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air. It is obvious that ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air are more capable of degrading methylene blue molecules. Fig. 7 shows the time variation of Ct/C0 during photocatalytic decolorization of methylene blue by ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air, where C0 is the initial concentration of methylene blue solution and Ct is the concentration of methylene blue solution at time t. Decolorization of methylene blue solution in the absence of any photocatalyst (only by irradiation) is also shown in Fig. 7 as the photolysis sample. Moreover, in order to have a benchmark on photocatalytic activities of the prepared samples, time-varied decolorization of methylene blue solution by a commercial ZnO powder is also shown. It is seen that photocatalytic activities of both samples synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air are higher than that of the commercial ZnO powder. However, the photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air is considerably higher than ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum.

It is well-known that the degradation of organic dyes is governed by the pseudo-first-order Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics [29] as follows:
(2)
C
t
=
C
0
e
-
kt
or
−
Ln
(
C
t
/
C
0
)
=
kt
where k is the degradation rate constant. Therefore, by plotting Ln(C0/Ct) versus time, the value of k is the slope of the fitted line. Fig. 7 (right) displays the plots of Ln(C0/Ct) for the corresponding data in time-varied degradation data in Fig. 7 (left). Furthermore, k (degradation rate constant) values related to photolysis, commercial ZnO powder, ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum, and ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air are shown in the inset of Fig. 7 (right). From the k values, the photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air is 2.77 times higher than that of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum.

Fig. 8 shows the recyclability of the photocatalytic activities ((1-C5h/C0) × 100) of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum and in ambient air in decolorization of methylene blue for six successive cycles, each after 5 h photocatalytic decolorization. It is seen that the photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air is nearly stable, except for a slight decrease from cycle 1 to cycle 2, which is probably due to permanent adsorption of methylene blue molecules on the nanorods. However, the photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum is not stable at all, which could be attributed to the aggregated form of ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum.

The successive reactions of thermal decomposition of zinc acetate dihydrate are as follows [15]:
(1)
Vaporization of water molecules confined in the zinc acetate dihydrate structure:
Zn(CH3COO)2 + 2H2O → Zn(CH3COO)2 + 2H2O ↑
(2)
Decomposition of some acetate molecules confined in the zinc acetate dihydrate structure into acetic acid (CH3COOH) molecules
4Zn(CH3COO)2 + 2H2O → Zn4O(CH3COO)6 + 2CH3COOH ↑
(3)
Decomposition of the previously produced zinc oxy-acetate molecules into ZnO molecules, acetic acid, acetone (CH3COCH3), and carbon dioxide
Zn4O(CH3COO)6 + 3H2O → 4ZnO + 6CH3COOH ↑
Zn4O(CH3COO)6 → 4ZnO + 3CH3COCH3 ↑ + 3CO2 ↑

The reactions above are all supposed self-sufficient in terms of oxygen supply. However, as ZnO molecules are naturally oxygen deficient, thermal decomposition of zinc acetate under vacuum and in ambient air can affect the extent of oxygen-deficiency of the obtained ZnO structures.

ZnO is a semiconductor; thus, when it is excited by photons of enough energy, it can generate electrons in its conduction band, leaving alone holes in its valence band. Since ZnO has a wide bandgap (~ 3.3 eV), the exciting photons must be in the UV region. The reaction is as follows:
ZnO + hν (E > Eg) → e- (Conduction band) + h+ (Valence band)

The generated electrons and holes have two main paths: (1) being transferred to the surfaces, or (2) being recombined with each other. Now, electrons and holes that have been transferred to surfaces interact with water molecules to produce superoxide (•O2 −) and hydroxyl radical (•OH) species through reductive and oxidative reactions. The hole-induced (oxidative) reactions are as follows:
h+ + H2O → H+ + •OH
2h+ + 2H2O → 2H+ + H2O2 → 2H+ + 2•OH

The electron-induced (reductive) reactions are as follows:
e- + O2 → •O2 -
•O2 − + 2HO• + H+ → H2O2 + O2
HOOH → HO• + •OH

Hydroxyl radicals, as the final products of both oxidative and reductive reactions, are highly oxidative, and they come into reaction with methylene blue molecules to decompose them into smaller fractions, as intermediate products such as amines, organic acids, etc., and finally into carbon dioxide and water molecules [30–32]. The reactions are as follows:
•OH + MB → MB-OH + •OH → … → MB-(OH)3
MB-(OH)3 → amines, organic acids, etc → … → H2O + CO2

The reasons behind the enhanced photocatalytic activity of ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air, as compared to ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum, can be categorized based on the results mentioned above: (1) they have a lower bandgap energy, so they can utilize a broader part of the light; (2) they have a higher absorption below 450 nm, so more photons can be absorbed to excite electrons to the conduction band to induce oxidative and reductive reactions; (3) they have a lower photoluminescence intensity, indicating their lower e-h recombination rate. In other words, electrons and holes would be recombined not faster than those of the sample synthesized under vacuum, which means that they have more time to participate in reactions with target molecules; (4) they are separated, not aggregated, as opposed to ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum, so they have a higher active surface area to participate in reactions (based on BET measurements).

In summary, ZnO nanorods were synthesized by thermal decomposition of zinc acetate, once under vacuum and once in ambient air, and the effect of the annealing environment on their photocatalytic activity for photodegradation of methylene blue was analyzed. It was found that the ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air have a higher crystallinity and a more specific surface area, and they are well separated. Moreover, it was shown that they have a smaller bandgap and a higher absorption below 450 nm. Further, they have a slightly lower PL intensity, indicating their lower e-h recombination rate, and a significantly higher ratio of intensities of the band-to-band peak to the green peak, suggesting their decreased defects and higher crystallinity. Finally, it was shown that ZnO nanorods synthesized in ambient air have a 2.77 times higher photocatalytic activity than ZnO nanorods synthesized under vacuum.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank research council of Tarbiat Modares University for providing financial support. The authors declare no competing interests.
